original_abstract: The transfer of health research data between the EU and the US remains a persistent challenge, shaped by the stringent requirements of the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and ongoing concerns regarding US mass surveillance. Of particular concern is the broad authority of US law enforcement and national security agencies to access personal data originating from the EU. Since the GDPR’s inception, a growing body of evidence suggests that these regulatory hurdles have significantly impeded transatlantic collaborations, especially those involving US public institutions. While a new EU-US adequacy decision was adopted in July 2023, it offers little guarantee of long-term stability, as it faces the looming threat of being challenged and potentially invalidated by the Court of Justice of the EU—repeating a cycle of legal uncertainty. As the research community urgently seeks sustainable solutions, the proposed European Health Data Space (EHDS) regulation may offer a new pathway to addressing these systemic issues. In this paper, we critically evaluate the existing legal framework and outline a strategic way forward to facilitate essential data transfers between public bodies.
humanized_abstract: As described in our previous reports, the transfer of health research data from the EU to the US continues to remain a problem. The restrictive GDPR rules combine with ongoing surveillance concerns around the world, in particular in relation to the vast amount of law enforcement and national security data collection authorities have in the US, over European citizens’ personal data. Since the entry into force of the GDPR, many cases have documented how partnerships between the EU and the US have fallen apart or become heavily impaired, especially with US universities and national research laboratories. Although the EU-US adequacy decision, which governed the international transfer of data between EU and US for decades, was replaced by a new one in July 2023, any stability ensured by this new decision will be short-lived, as many lawyers already predict it will be annulled, in the same way as the previous decision, by the EU Court of Justice. Meanwhile, the EHDS regulation which is under discussion at EU level might perhaps be a way to put an end to once and for all to these data transfer controversies between public entities. This policy brief examines the limitations of current EU law in relation to the transfer of personal health data between public entities for health research purposes and draws a roadmap for overcoming the obstacles to data sharing that presently observed in practice.
